one_line_takeaway: A server-only dynamic aggregation scheme that clusters FL clients by class-probability behavior and weights them toward an identity-matrix ideal, substantially improving rare-attack detection and poisoning robustness in IoT IDS at negligible device cost.
key_finding: On multi-class attack classification, FedMADE sharply raises per-class accuracy for minority attacks that other FL methods largely miss: at gamma=1.0 web-based attack accuracy reaches 58.5% (CNN) / 69.5% (FCNN) and brute-force 51.4% (CNN) / 47.1% (FCNN), versus baselines that fail entirely or reach only up to 13.8%; at gamma=0.5 minority-attack classification accuracy rises to 71.07%. Binary detection is on par with FedAvg (F1 > 0.99 for both). It is robust to poisoning: under 35% data-poisoned devices, FedAvg attack/benign per-class accuracy collapses to 94.44%/23.22% while FedMADE holds at 98.20%/98.35%. Latency overhead vs FedAvg is small (+4.7% / 5.03s at gamma=0.5; +2.6% at gamma=1.0), far below FedProx (+21-23%) and SCAFFOLD (+50%), and unlike those two it adds no device-side compute.
core_contribution: FedMADE, a dynamic model-aggregation method that runs ENTIRELY on the federated-learning (FL) central server to improve minority-attack classification in FL-based IoT intrusion detection systems (IDSs). Its own novelty is the aggregation step, not the local models: (1) for each client's local model, the server computes a Class Probability Matrix (CPM) by averaging softmax output vectors per class over a tiny auxiliary validation set (10 samples/class); (2) DBSCAN clusters clients by CPM similarity so similarly-performing models are grouped, reducing scaling cost; (3) per-cluster averaged CPMs (CCPMs) feed an optimization that picks non-negative aggregation weights alpha_k minimizing the L2 distance between the weighted sum of CCPMs and the identity matrix (the ideal 'always correct with 100% confidence' matrix), so consistently accurate models get larger weight and poisoned/weak models get near-zero weight. Because it only reweights aggregation, it adds no local compute to IoT devices and does not modify local training. Note: FedProx, SCAFFOLD, FedAvg, DBSCAN, SMOTE, and CICIoT2023 are cited prior work used as baselines/tools, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Two roles. (1) Data-privacy motivation: centralized ML IDSs require raw traffic to leave devices; FL keeps data local. (2) Adversarial robustness: a subset of IoT clients is compromised and tries to steer the global model to misclassify traffic. The paper evaluates data poisoning (label flipping: attack->benign and benign->attack, 5%-40% of devices compromised) and model poisoning (5% compromised clients upload weights scaled by factor lambda in [5,40]). FedMADE defends by assigning negligible aggregation weight to models whose CPMs deviate from benign clients. The heterogeneity problem it primarily targets (device-specific class imbalance) is a robustness/utility issue, not itself an attack.
limitations: Minority-attack accuracy, while much improved, is still only ~47-71% (not near-perfect), and FedMADE slightly reduces accuracy for benign and reconnaissance traffic due to base-model confusion among benign/recon/web/brute-force classes; the authors tried several feature-selection/extraction methods (variance, information gain, RFE, tree-based, PCA, autoencoder) without meaningful gains and defer better feature engineering as out of scope. The method relies on a server-side auxiliary labeled validation set (10 samples/class), which must be representative. Evaluated on a single dataset (CICIoT2023) with two simple base models; the identity-matrix optimization target assumes classes are separable. Web-based attacks touch only 3 of 63 devices, so per-round accuracy can drop to zero when those devices are absent, mitigated only by keeping the best historical model.

datasets_used: CICIoT2023 (105 real IoT devices; 63 victim clients; 33 attacks in 7 categories: DDoS, DoS, Mirai, Recon, Spoofing, Web-based, Brute Force; 47 features via DPKT)
Server-side auxiliary validation set: 10 samples per class (used to compute CPMs)
quantitative_results: Multi-class per-class accuracy, gamma=1.0 CNN: web-based 58.5%, brute-force 51.4%
Multi-class per-class accuracy, gamma=1.0 FCNN: web-based 69.5%, brute-force 47.1%
Baselines (FedAvg/FedProx/SCAFFOLD) reach only up to 13.8% or fail entirely on minority attacks
gamma=0.5: minority-attack classification accuracy up to 71.07%
Binary detection F1 > 0.99 for both FedMADE and FedAvg (e.g. CNN gamma=1.0: FedMADE P0.9990/R0.9931/F0.9960/Acc0.9923 vs FedAvg P0.9982/R0.9947/F0.9965/Acc0.9931)
Robustness at 35% data-poisoned devices (CNN): FedAvg attack/benign accuracy 94.44%/23.22%, FedMADE 98.20%/98.35%
Class imbalance: minority web-based+brute-force = 0.08% of flows; DoS/DDoS/Mirai = 95.76%
Latency round times: FedAvg 107.46s (gamma=0.5)/221.82s (gamma=1.0); FedMADE 112.49s(+4.7%)/227.66s(+2.6%); FedProx +23.3%/+21.5%; SCAFFOLD +50.2%/+50.5%
5.03s absolute per-round overhead vs FedAvg at gamma=0.5 (31 vs 63 local models per round at gamma 0.5 vs 1.0)
baselines_compared: FedAvg (McMahan et al. 2017) - default dataset-size-weighted aggregation
FedProx (Li et al. 2020) - proximal term on local loss
